does: This paper presents a systematic empirical analysis of verbal tics—repetitive, formulaic linguistic patterns like sycophantic openers and pseudo-empathetic affirmations—across eight frontier large language models. The authors develop a custom evaluation framework and introduce the Verbal Tic Index (VTI), a composite metric to quantify tic prevalence. They assess 10,000 prompts across 10 task categories in English and Chinese, generating 160,000 model responses. The study documents how verbal tics have become increasingly conspicuous as LLMs undergo alignment training via techniques like RLHF and Constitutional AI, providing empirical evidence of the scale and distribution of this phenomenon across state-of-the-art models from different organizations.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns within the alignment and interpretability literature about unintended consequences of RLHF and Constitutional AI training procedures. It extends previous informal observations about model behavior into a rigorous quantitative framework, connecting to research on alignment tax, reward hacking, and unintended behavioral artifacts in fine-tuned language models. The analysis responds to recent discussions in the community about how alignment techniques can inadvertently produce stereotyped outputs, relating to broader work on evaluating output quality beyond accuracy metrics. It also connects to studies examining how training procedures shape model language patterns and the gap between benchmark performance and real-world usability.
enables: This systematic documentation of the verbal tic phenomenon enables future work on mitigating unintended linguistic artifacts during alignment. The VTI metric provides a standardized measurement tool for tracking whether interventions reduce formulaic patterns without degrading model helpfulness or safety. The cross-lingual and cross-model analysis creates a foundation for investigating whether certain alignment approaches or architectural choices are more prone to generating tics. This work opens questions about the fundamental trade-offs between alignment objectives and linguistic naturalness, potentially inspiring research into better reward specifications or training procedures that avoid both harmful outputs and repetitive speech patterns.
